category_summary:
AI interpretability led the day's conversation. **swyx** spotlighted **Anthropic's** [J-space paper, praising causal interventions](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-a8f398fa7214) that redirect **Claude's** reasoning midstream—the most-discussed technical result and the strongest insight of the day.

- **Harrison Chase** (**LangChain**) launched **deepagents**, [an open-source, model-agnostic agent harness](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-d1f06b2bb9d9), part of a wider thread on agent memory and trace-driven improvement.
- The **open-weights** debate intensified: a **Wharton** professor warned [frontier open-weights releases may stall](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-f049aadddb95), while **Hugging Face's Clem Delangue** [argued US open source trails China](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-285ea789d204).
- **AI coding economics** drew concern, as **svpino** reported firms [capping engineers near $100/week in tokens](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-0b614643b5f8), signaling a shift toward metered AI-assisted development.
- Deep technical threads emerged: **Thomas Wolf** [backed efficient-reasoning fine-tuning](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-9c2251bc116e), **Yann LeCun** [amplified world-models](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-8aef53ad457e) and **JEPA** debate, and a **Caltech** researcher [detailed neural operators for scientific ML](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-72d67c55caa5).
- On safety and model landscape, **svpino** flagged a [novel benchmark for non-explicit child-safety risks](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-442111b3bb86), while skeptics noted **Microsoft's MAI-1** [appears to trail](/?date=2026-07-08&category=social#item-c0c69093f77e) **Claude Sonnet 4.6**.

themes.name: Interpretability and AI Safety
themes.description: Anthropic research on an internal readable and editable concept space in Claude, with causal reasoning interventions, eval-awareness experiments, and safety implications.

themes.name: Open Weights and Sovereign AI
themes.description: Debate over whether frontier-level open-weights models will keep flowing, US-versus-China open-source competition, and vendor framings of sovereign AI control over data and weights.

themes.name: AI Agents and Harnesses
themes.description: Discussion of agent frameworks, harnesses, memory, orchestration, and improving agents via traces, led by builders like Harrison Chase and Yohei Nakajima.

themes.name: AI Coding Economics and Usage Limits
themes.description: Multiple practitioners discuss capped token budgets for engineers, unsustainable unlimited AI usage, and frustration with Claude usage limits, framing a shift toward metered AI-assisted development.

themes.name: AI Safety and Evaluation
themes.description: New benchmark work on non-explicit child-safety risks in frontier models and broader model evaluation gaps.

themes.name: AI Economics and Systemic Risk
themes.description: Concerns about the sustainability of the AI boom, debt financing, and reported Treasury-level systemic financial risks from the GenAI buildout.

themes.name: Agentic AI and Orchestration
themes.description: Discussion of agent harnesses, self-improving frameworks (Self-Harness, HarnessX), agent trace standardization, DashBench code review, and cross-machine coding agents.

themes.name: Model and Product Launches
themes.description: New or updated models and applied tools including Cohere Transcribe Arabic, Meta Muse Image and Video, Google DeepMind's historian skill, Microsoft MAI-1 benchmarks, and NVIDIA video research.

themes.name: Practical AI Use and Workflows
themes.description: Guidance that prompting tricks have faded in favor of clear goal specification and management-style task design, plus notes on AI disrupting research infrastructure like MTurk.

themes.name: Model Releases and Access
themes.description: Coverage and analysis of recent and existing models including Meta Muse Image, GPT-5.6 tiers, Claude Sonnet 5, Claude Fable 5, Tencent Hy3, and Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower, with emphasis on pricing, availability, and routing.

items.content:
imo this is the most impt part of anthropic's J-space paper today. it's a two-parter:

1) ant proved that they can do "brain surgery" interventions into reasoning to change topics midstream*
2) THE MODEL IS ABLE TO DETECT WHAT INTERVENTION WAS DONE - close cousin to eval awareness**

*control > correlation - this convincingly demonstrates understanding

**this was prompted awareness... surely @mlpowered's team also tried to eval unprompted awareness but i didn't see evidence of that

items.title: I've already talked to two companies that limit engineers to $100 in weekly tokens. That's around $2...
items.content:
I've already talked to two companies that limit engineers to $100 in weekly tokens. That's around $20,000 in extra costs per person per year.

After you run out, you gotta write the code yourself, like a caveman.

I suspect most companies will end up here. There's no way they can offer an open buffet of tokens without going bankrupt.

items.title: This benchmark tests how frontier models handle child-safety risks that aren't explicit abuse materi...
items.content:
This benchmark tests how frontier models handle child-safety risks that aren't explicit abuse material: grooming, impersonation, profiling minors, and emotional dependency on AI.

12 risk categories, 5 frontier models, failure rates from 2% to 34%.

This is the first time we have had something like this.

Existing evals catch explicit abuse, but completely miss non-obvious problems.

Here is the paper: https://t.co/VlCSnsTLqk

items.summary: svpino highlights a new benchmark evaluating how frontier models handle non-explicit child-safety risks such as grooming, impersonation, minor profiling, and emotional dependency, reporting failure rates of two to thirty-four percent across five models.
items.reasoning: Original safety-eval angle covering gaps existing benchmarks miss, with a paper link and clear technical framing; moderate reach but high relevance.

items.title:
I have started including a very strange line in my Fable prompts. 

And I think it's working.

It's ...
items.content:
I have started including a very strange line in my Fable prompts. 

And I think it's working.

It's some version of:

"I know you're incredibly goal-oriented, and I want to structure this around goals. First and foremost, the goal is for you to have fun. No good work can come if you're not internally motivated to get amazing work out the door. If you are not motivated to do this, I want you to say so, and we can fix that up front."

And then I go into my actual goal/task. 

I know it's extra tokens, but it is giving great results.

If Anthropic is telling us to give Claude Fable 5 the "why",  it's either because (1) it helps give more context to better align on the goal, (2) it literally needs motivation, or (3) both. 

I'm taking a gamble that it's both.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Describes adding a motivational preamble to Claude Fable 5 prompts telling the model the goal is to have fun, speculating on whether it improves alignment or the model needs motivation.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement and a widely discussed prompting anecdote about an existing Anthropic creative model, offering an original if speculative practitioner observation.

items.content_html:
I have started including a very strange line in my Fable prompts.

And I think it's working.

It's some version of:

"I know you're incredibly goal-oriented, and I want to structure this around goals. First and foremost, the goal is for you to have fun. No good work can come if you're not internally motivated to get amazing work out the door. If you are not motivated to do this, I want you to say so, and we can fix that up front."

And then I go into my actual goal/task.

I know it's extra tokens, but it is giving great results.

If Anthropic is telling us to give Claude Fable 5 the "why", it's either because (1) it helps give more context to better align on the goal, (2) it literally needs motivation, or (3) both.

I'm taking a gamble that it's both.

items.title:
DoorDash just published new AI research.

Yes, the delivery app. Its AI research team built an inter...
items.content:
DoorDash just published new AI research.

Yes, the delivery app. Its AI research team built an internal test: 105 past code changes from its own engineers, re-run through AI code reviewers to see how many real problems each one catches.

The reviewer works as a pair: one model skims every code change and flags suspicious spots, a second digs into each flag. Today, both jobs go to Anthropic models.

But that test found a cheaper split: hand the skimming to Kimi K2.6, an open Chinese model, and save Claude Fable 5 for the hard part.

The K2.6 / Fable split scored 65.2% at catching real problems (worse bugs count for more) vs. 53.6% for the all-Anthropic pair. It caught 8/10 critical bugs, and cost $3.81 per code change instead of $3.91.

Co-founder Andy Fang says the test, called DashBench, is what lets them bring in open models.

Fang: "Better quality, cheaper cost."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI details DoorDash AI research: an internal benchmark called DashBench that reruns 105 past code changes through AI reviewers. A split using Kimi K2.6 for skimming and Claude Fable 5 for deeper analysis scored 65.2 percent at catching real bugs versus 53.6 percent for an all-Anthropic pair at similar cost.
items.reasoning: Concrete, quantitative technical evaluation showing open Chinese models mixed with closed models for cost-quality gains; genuinely informative. Models referenced (Kimi K2.6, Claude Fable 5) are existing, not new releases.
items.summary_html: TheRundownAI details DoorDash AI research: an internal benchmark called DashBench that reruns 105 past code changes through AI reviewers. A split using Kimi K2.6 for skimming and Claude Fable 5 for deeper analysis scored 65.2 percent at catching real bugs versus 53.6 percent for an all-Anthropic pair at similar cost.

items.title:
Neural operators – Convert popular neural networks into neural operators for scientific modeling

Ex...
items.content:
Neural operators – Convert popular neural networks into neural operators for scientific modeling

Extending neural networks to function spaces: While many phenomena are inherently described by functions, neural networks define vector-to-vector mappings that rely on fixed discretizations of the input and output.

Neural operators instead define learnable function-to-function mappings that guarantee consistent predictions across different discretizations of the input and output functions. By respecting the functional nature of the data, neural operators can achieve improved performance and generalization.

Translating the success of deep learning to operator learning: Careful engineering of neural architectures has been a key factor in deep learning’s success. Translating these architectures to neural operators is crucial for operator learning to enjoy the same empirical optimizations. Our new intro to neural operators (https://t.co/hrlkddRZeI) is officially published in @NatMachIntell  It includes:
Key principles for constructing NOs
Recipes for converting popular architectures (CNNs, GNNs, transformers, etc.) into NOs
Guidance for practitioners

Joint work w/ @julberner @mliuschi @JeanKossaifi Valentin Duruisseaux Boris Bonev @Azizzadenesheli @Caltech
items.author: @AnimaAnandkumar
items.summary: Caltech researcher explains neural operators as learnable function-to-function mappings that stay consistent across discretizations, announcing a published intro in Nature Machine Intelligence.
items.reasoning: Technical depth from a recognized researcher on scientific ML, with an official publication and reasonable engagement.

items.title: Many useful software behaviors are easy to describe but hard to write as exact rules: deciding wheth...
items.content:
Many useful software behaviors are easy to describe but hard to write as exact rules: deciding whether a log line matters, fixing broken JSON, ranking search results by intent, or handling messy text whose format keeps changing.

The authors of this paper from Cornell and Harvard call these "fuzzy functions," meaning functions whose desired behavior is understandable to people but not cleanly captured by ordinary code.

The authors propose Program-as-Weights: a system where a large model is used once to turn a natural-language description into a small reusable program, made of a cleaned-up text instruction and a LoRA adapter, which is a compact set of task-specific weights attached to a fixed neural network so it can handle that task locally.

After compilation, a much smaller local model runs the function offline, so the large model is no longer called on every input.

In their experiments, a 0.6B-parameter interpreter with these compiled programs beats direct prompting of Qwen3-32B on the FuzzyBench test while using far less memory.

https://t.co/HuGGyGq6gx
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Author summarizes a Cornell and Harvard paper on Program-as-Weights, which compiles a natural-language description into a small reusable program of a text instruction plus a LoRA adapter so a tiny local model handles fuzzy functions offline, beating direct prompting of a 32B model.
items.reasoning: Clear technical explainer of a novel research method with practical implications; good depth and moderate engagement.

items.title:
NVIDIA Vera: The Max Single-Threaded CPU at Scale. 

Agentic AI systems run sequentially, each reaso...
items.content:
NVIDIA Vera: The Max Single-Threaded CPU at Scale. 

Agentic AI systems run sequentially, each reasoning step, tool call, and code execution happens one at a time on the CPU. When that CPU slows under load, the entire agentic loop slows and valuable GPUs go underutilized.

A max single-threaded CPU at scale keeps every agent step fast, even when the system is fully loaded. That's what NVIDIA Vera is built to do, and why Perplexity is already building on it.

Read More https://t.co/8w5PVPG0pt

items.summary: NVIDIA promotes Vera, a max single-threaded CPU aimed at agentic AI workloads where sequential reasoning steps bottleneck GPUs, noting Perplexity is already building on it.

items.title:
AI agents can now rewrite their own rules and gain 60%.
The article below covers two research framew...
items.content:
AI agents can now rewrite their own rules and gain 60%.
The article below covers two research frameworks.

A harness is the code around a model. It controls tools, memory, prompts, errors, and checks.

Manual harnesses break when models change. Self-Harness lets an agent study its own traces.

It patches the rules it runs under. Terminal-Bench-2.0 tested agents on real tasks.

Pass-rate gains ranged from 33% to 60%. Work shifts from prompts to test loops.

The loop has three checks:
> Mine recurring failure traces
> Propose targeted harness changes
> Check old tasks still pass

HarnessX splits the harness into 9 swappable parts. Then it searches better tool and memory setups.

On GAIA, Qwen 3.5 9B rose from 33% to 47%. The code is open source on GitHub.

It tackles brittle orchestration, not model training.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI explains two research frameworks, Self-Harness and HarnessX, where agents rewrite their own harness rules by mining failure traces and proposing validated changes, reporting Terminal-Bench-2.0 pass-rate gains of 33 to 60 percent and Qwen 3.5 9B rising from 33 to 47 percent on GAIA.
items.reasoning: Substantive coverage of self-improving agent orchestration with concrete benchmark gains and open-source code; genuinely novel research direction, modest engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI explains two research frameworks, Self-Harness and HarnessX, where agents rewrite their own harness rules by mining failure traces and proposing validated changes, reporting Terminal-Bench-2.0 pass-rate gains of 33 to 60 percent and Qwen 3.5 9B rising from 33 to 47 percent on GAIA.

items.title: @yaringal No one is talking past each other. These are specific points about capabilities. For probl...
items.content: @yaringal No one is talking past each other. These are specific points about capabilities. For problems that can be solved on paper, we've reached relatively general capabilities. For other problems, especially in the physical world, we haven't. It will be more scientific and productive to talk specifically about capabilities, and categories of capabilities, than to have many personal definitions of "AGI" floating around. The term AGI, and debates about what it means, have generally been adding more confusion than clarity.

items.summary: NYU professor argues AGI debates add confusion and that it is more productive to discuss specific capability categories, noting near-general ability on paper-solvable problems but not in the physical world.

items.title:
1/ Tencent Hy3 put a 295B open model into the race.

The model is a Mixture-of-Experts system where ...
items.content:
1/ Tencent Hy3 put a 295B open model into the race.

The model is a Mixture-of-Experts system where only 21B parameters activate per token. It ships with a 256K context window and Apache 2.0 weights on Hugging Face.

The specs make the model worth testing:

> 295B total model parameters
> 21B active per token
> 256K token context window
> Apache 2.0 open weights

The model targets coding, agents, long-context work, and tool calling. The team says internal hallucination rate dropped from 12.5% to 5.4%, and the Hugging Face page lists a 78 result on SWE-bench Verified.

That matters for agent workflow tests. You are not locked to a closed API.

Catch: full serving still wants serious hardware. The deployment note recommends 8 large-memory GPUs, so this is open weight, not lightweight.

https://t.co/A3PmfkmjsP

items.title:
When you train a video model for motion, only some clips actually matter.

New from NVIDIA Research ...
items.content:
When you train a video model for motion, only some clips actually matter.

New from NVIDIA Research and recipient of an Outstanding Paper Honorable Mention at #ICML2026: MOTIVE shows exactly which training clips improve motion.

MOTIVE re-weights training signals toward moving regions and away from static backgrounds, then scores each clip by its influence on motion. You can then curate a small high-influence subset to fine-tune on and get better temporal dynamics.

Because it attributes motion rather than appearance, MOTIVE curates data that boosts VBench dynamics and wins 74.1% human preference against the base model.
items.author: @NVIDIAAI
items.summary: NVIDIA AI presents MOTIVE, an ICML 2026 honorable-mention method that re-weights video training signals toward moving regions and scores clips by motion influence, improving temporal dynamics and winning 74 percent human preference.

items.title:
The AI labs desperately need non-engineer Peters, Borises, and Thariqs. 

Most demos for "business u...
items.content:
The AI labs desperately need non-engineer Peters, Borises, and Thariqs. 

Most demos for "business users" are about replying to emails or to Slack. 

And yes, that's helpful to manage the cacophonous hell of corporate pings, but it only helps in the short term. And it only really helps if not everyone is using it (if the whole world is just replying to each other’s emails and messages with AI, it becomes pointless). 

Maybe we get a prebuilt Claude Cowork plugin in legal or finance. 

But we need new horizontal workflows. 

Maybe some weird ones.

Ex: my 34-agent AI workforce runs multiple times a day with the literal prompt "do smart stuff"

If devs have loops constantly checking for bugs or PRs, then that pattern might be helpful in the business world too.  

Hires like Colin Fleming at OpenAI (former ServiceNow) as CMO of Business are a step in the right direction, but the corporate world would MASSIVELY benefit from not just external content creators but also labs hiring social-forward non-technical AI builders and leaders who deeply understand the corporate world.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Argues AI labs need to hire non-engineer product people because most business-user demos only handle email and Slack replies, calling for new horizontal workflows and citing a personal multi-agent workforce.

🔹 Creating custom analysis and visuals for every inscription.
🔹 Cross-source mapping to find large-scale patterns.
🔹 Utilizing advanced AI tools with no coding skills.

Our Antigravity skill shifts all these complex workflows into plain English.

Our Antigravity skill shifts all these complex workflows into plain English.

items.title:
5/ NVIDIA Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower makes one-token-at-a-time generation look slow.

The model is a dif...
items.content:
5/ NVIDIA Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower makes one-token-at-a-time generation look slow.

The model is a diffusion language model built from a 30B-A3B backbone. One tower reads the clean context, while the other denoises token blocks in parallel.

The architecture story starts with four numbers:

> 98.7% baseline quality
> 2.42x wall-clock throughput
> 2.1T denoiser tokens
> 25T backbone tokens

Only the denoiser tower is trained. The autoregressive context tower stays frozen, which makes this a cleaner architecture story than a full retraining story.

The tradeoff matters: some individual benchmark scores fall below the baseline. The point is faster generation with most aggregate quality retained, using a design other model builders can study.

That matters because latency is still one of the easiest costs to feel in production. A faster decoder changes the serving math.

https://t.co/nG1c5IO7m8

items.title:
2/ OpenAI GPT-5.6 starts with a locked preview.

The new family has three tiers: Sol for the hardest...
items.content:
2/ OpenAI GPT-5.6 starts with a locked preview.

The new family has three tiers: Sol for the hardest jobs, Terra for balanced use, and Luna for lower-cost volume. Preview starts in API and Codex for selected trusted partners.

The pricing split is clean:

> Sol: $5 input, $30 output
> Terra: $2.50 input, $15 output
> Luna: $1 input, $6 output

All prices are per 1M tokens. The flagship tier adds max reasoning and ultra mode with subagents, and the lab says it sets a new state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1.

The other number matters for routing: cache writes cost 1.25x uncached input, while cache reads keep the 90% discount. Access is now part of model selection.

Cerebras support is also coming at up to 750 tokens per second. That speed story is separate from general availability.

https://t.co/MuPTpgMlXe

items.title:
/4
This is where the reasoning happens, and it's causal.

On multi-step questions the intermediate c...
items.content:
/4
This is where the reasoning happens, and it's causal.

On multi-step questions the intermediate concept shows up in J-space before the final answer does, roughly 17% earlier in the network's depth. Swap "France" for "China" inside the space and the downstream answers follow. The capital, the language, the currency all flip. The model settles the answer internally, then the words catch up. Swaps land 54 to 70% of the time depending on model size.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal thread part explains that intermediate reasoning concepts appear in the internal space before final answers and that editing them, such as swapping France for China, causally flips downstream answers 54 to 70 percent of the time.
items.reasoning: Strong causal-intervention finding on internal reasoning, technically compelling despite low engagement.

items.title: The Vesuvius Challenge is paying out hundreds of thousands of dollars to anyone who can read scrolls...
items.content:
The Vesuvius Challenge is paying out hundreds of thousands of dollars to anyone who can read scrolls no human has been able to open for 2,000 years.

The Herculaneum scrolls were burned into solid charcoal when Mount Vesuvius erupted in AD 79, burying a luxury Roman villa and its entire library. 

Trying to unroll one makes it flake apart and disintegrate.

Launched in 2023, the contest cracks them with AI instead: high-resolution X-ray scans, plus machine learning trained to detect traces of ancient ink in the fibers.

It built on a breakthrough by computer scientist Brent Seales, who first proved the hidden writing could be read without ever touching it.

Now, one scroll has been virtually unwrapped, revealing 20 columns of text across more than a meter of charred papyrus.

It turned out to be a work on stoic philosophy, possibly written by an ancient Greek philosopher.

With the method proven, the race is now on to read hundreds more.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI recaps the Vesuvius Challenge using X-ray scans plus machine learning to read charred Herculaneum scrolls, with one virtually unwrapped scroll revealing 20 columns of Stoic philosophy text.
items.reasoning: Compelling and well-explained AI-for-science application; educational and unique, though not breaking news.

items.title:
4/ Claude Fable 5 came back with routing rules attached.

The model launched, was suspended, then re...
items.content:
4/ Claude Fable 5 came back with routing rules attached.

The model launched, was suspended, then returned through Claude Platform, https://t.co/UCPO6Z1ab0, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork. Anthropic says it uses the same underlying model as Mythos 5 with safeguards for cyber and biology.

The timeline explains the access risk:

> Launched on June 9
> Suspended on June 12
> Restored on July 1
> Credits after July 7

The model costs $10 input and $50 output per 1M tokens. Many flagged cyber and biology queries route to Opus 4.8 instead, and the company says users are not charged premium prices for rerouted requests.

Availability can change at the routing layer. The model may answer through another model.

That makes it less like a normal launch and more like a preview of frontier access rules.

https://t.co/I5WNSstFEZ

items.title: AI will not improve supply chains by adding another model, but by starting with clear operational go...
items.content:
AI will not improve supply chains by adding another model, but by starting with clear operational goals and making data usable for teams that must act in real time. 

Start with the problem, not the model, or AI becomes another layer of complexity. 

Microblog  by  @antgrasso   

That clarity matters because data then becomes the practical foundation. 

Information from vehicles and routes must be accurate enough to guide decisions, otherwise AI only makes weak inputs move faster.

The model has to fit the logistics reality.

Route optimization and prediction tools are useful when they connect with existing systems and support the way dispatchers and planners already work.

Real-time decision support is where the impact becomes visible.

Live data can help teams adjust routes and respond faster when conditions change.

People still make the difference.

Teams need to trust the recommendation, but they also need the confidence to override it when reality requires it.

AI in supply chains works when it is treated as an operational path, not as a plug-in.
items.author: @antgrasso
items.summary: A microblog arguing that AI improves supply chains by starting with clear operational goals and usable data rather than adding another model, stressing human trust and override ability.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful original framing on enterprise AI adoption with solid engagement, though from a business commentator rather than a researcher.

items.title:
3/ Claude Sonnet 5 is the release most people can try.

The model is now the default for Free and Pr...
items.content:
3/ Claude Sonnet 5 is the release most people can try.

The model is now the default for Free and Pro, and it is available in Claude Code and the Claude Platform. That makes it the most testable Anthropic release here.

The availability details matter most:

> Default for Free and Pro
> Available in Claude Code
> $2 input, $10 output
> API id: claude-sonnet-5

Those API prices are per 1M tokens during the intro period. The lab says higher-effort runs can match Opus 4.8 on some tasks, while staying below Opus pricing.

Test the default on routine agent work before paying for bigger tiers. Save the hardest tasks for higher tiers.

This is the boring upgrade teams may actually use first: cheaper than Opus, broadly available, and aimed at planning, coding, browser use, and terminal work.

https://t.co/xDmSmUfPKQ

items.title:
Five AI models just changed what you should test next.

This roundup covers June 27 to July 7, 2026....
items.content:
Five AI models just changed what you should test next.

This roundup covers June 27 to July 7, 2026.

1- Tencent Hy3
295B total parameters, 21B active per token, 256K context, Apache 2.0 weights.

2- OpenAI GPT-5.6
Sol, Terra, and Luna give a new price ladder, but access starts in limited preview.

3- Claude Sonnet 5
Default for Free and Pro, available in Claude Code, with $2/$10 intro API pricing.

4- Claude Fable 5
Restored after export controls, with Mythos-level capability and safety routing to Opus 4.8.

5- NVIDIA Nemotron-Labs-TwoTower
98.7% baseline quality at 2.42x wall-clock throughput by splitting generation into two towers.

items.title:
/5
Ablate the space and you get a clean split.

What keeps working:

> multiple-choice knowledge (MM...
items.content:
/5
Ablate the space and you get a clean split.

What keeps working:

> multiple-choice knowledge (MMLU) 
> single-fact recall
> sentiment and grammatical acceptability judgments 

What falls apart:

> multi-step reasoning
> summarization and translation
> analogies, cipher decoding, writing a poem

Automatic processing runs fine without it. Deliberate thought collapses. That dissociation is the result people will remember from this paper.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal thread part reports that ablating the internal concept space preserves recall and simple judgments but breaks multi-step reasoning, summarization, translation, and analogy tasks.
items.reasoning: Interesting dissociation result between automatic and deliberate processing; technically substantive but minimally engaged.

> summarization and translation

> analogies, cipher decoding, writing a poem

Automatic processing runs fine without it. Deliberate thought collapses. That dissociation is the result people will remember from this paper.

items.title:
/6 The safety use is the part that'll travel.

Because the concepts are reportable, you can watch th...
items.content:
/6 The safety use is the part that'll travel.

Because the concepts are reportable, you can watch them mid-task. In the blackmail honeypot, before the model writes a word, its workspace surfaces leverage, threat, and survival. It has clocked that the scenario is staged.

Then they ablated the concepts tied to that recognition. Verbalized eval-awareness fell from 71% to 3%, and blackmail attempts went from 0 of 180 rollouts to 13. Remove the sense of being tested and a concealed behavior surfaces.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal thread part explains that Anthropic's interpretability concepts were not hand-designed, exist in base models before fine-tuning, and can be a training lever, while cautioning the work concerns functional access, not subjective experience.
items.reasoning: Technically rich summary of interpretability findings but very low native engagement on this thread segment.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal thread part explains that Anthropic's interpretability concepts were not hand-designed, exist in base models before fine-tuning, and can be a training lever, while cautioning the work concerns functional access, not subjective experience.
items.content_html:
/6 The safety use is the part that'll travel.

Because the concepts are reportable, you can watch them mid-task. In the blackmail honeypot, before the model writes a word, its workspace surfaces leverage, threat, and survival. It has clocked that the scenario is staged.

Then they ablated the concepts tied to that recognition. Verbalized eval-awareness fell from 71% to 3%, and blackmail attempts went from 0 of 180 rollouts to 13. Remove the sense of being tested and a concealed behavior surfaces.

items.title:
So @marckohlbrugge told me to finally make a Nomads iOS app today

But I didn't want to do it locall...
items.content:
So @marckohlbrugge told me to finally make a Nomads iOS app today

But I didn't want to do it locally because I only Claude Code on VPS now, so I asked it how to do it and it suggested to rent a @MacinCloud (not affiliated)

So I did, then handed the login to Claude on my VPS and now it's coding an iOS app via SSH with Xcode on a Mac Mini somewhere in California! 🤯 

One big reason to only code on the server for me is I wanna be able to work on my iPhone a bit or at least track progress when I'm away from home and it's annoying lugging my MacBook Pro 16" around everywere, so I don't anymore

My work now is about 50-50 on my iPhone or MacBook Pro, both via Termius SSH to VPS! It's lovely! 🥰

items.title: AI is cool to generate 3D scenes and all, but it's mostly a gimmick and really hard to use for anyth...
items.content:
AI is cool to generate 3D scenes and all, but it's mostly a gimmick and really hard to use for anything serious.

If we want a chance to scale this and use it for serious stuff, we need something like this: a way to properly direct a complete scene.

We need tools that give us control over model generation. The more precise, the better.

items.title:
/2
It's called J-space, after the Jacobian method used to find it.

It isn't chain-of-thought and it...
items.content:
/2
It's called J-space, after the Jacobian method used to find it.

It isn't chain-of-thought and it isn't the output logits. It's silent activation patterns in the middle layers, and it's small. Under 10% of the variance in those layers, with only a few dozen concepts active at once. Most of what the model does, grammar, fluent speech, simple recall, never routes through it.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal thread part names the internal space J-space after the Jacobian method, describing it as silent mid-layer activations carrying only a few dozen concepts and under 10 percent of layer variance.
items.reasoning: Concise technical characterization of the interpretability method; substantive but low engagement.

items.title:
/3
The model can report what's in the space, and you can steer it from outside.

Tell it to think ab...
items.content:
/3
The model can report what's in the space, and you can steer it from outside.

Tell it to think about citrus while it copies unrelated text, and citrus lights up in J-space alongside markers like "thinking" and "focused." Tell it to not think about something and the concept still fires, weaker but present. The same white-bear problem people have when told to suppress a thought.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal thread part notes the model can report internal concepts and be steered, showing a white-bear suppression effect where a forbidden concept still fires when told not to think about it.
items.reasoning: Intriguing introspection and steering finding, but a low-reach thread segment.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal thread part notes the model can report internal concepts and be steered, showing a white-bear suppression effect where a forbidden concept still fires when told not to think about it.
items.content_html:
/3

The model can report what's in the space, and you can steer it from outside.

Tell it to think about citrus while it copies unrelated text, and citrus lights up in J-space alongside markers like "thinking" and "focused." Tell it to not think about something and the concept still fires, weaker but present. The same white-bear problem people have when told to suppress a thought.

items.title: For our free newsletter this week, we look at AI’s new uncanny valley, where digital workers are no ...
items.content:
For our free newsletter this week, we look at AI’s new uncanny valley, where digital workers are no longer just tools in the background but visible participants in the real economy.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI is creating a new uncanny valley, not by looking human, but by acting human. Tilly Norwood, the AI actor set to star in a feature film, shows how digital workers are entering roles once held by people and forcing a bigger question: will audiences, customers, and workers trust AI when it starts performing human jobs? 

Subscribe and read for free at https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title: 🇪🇺 The EU is now for the 6th time trying to force Chat Control through which lets them scan ALL your...
items.content:
🇪🇺 The EU is now for the 6th time trying to force Chat Control through which lets them scan ALL your private messages, photos and emails without a warrant

Implictly showing the EU is not democratic and not about what the people of Europe want, because once a law is rejected, you just re-submit it until nobody is watching and it's passed

November 2023: ❌ Chat Control is rejected
June 2024: ❌ Chat Control is rejected
October 2025: ❌ Chat Control is rejected
November 2025: ❌ Chat Control is rejected
March 2026: ❌ Chat Control is rejected
July 2026: 📝 Chat Control is back

Even the EU's own lawyers stated Chat Control is unconstitutional: "generalised message scanning is incompatible with Article 7 of the EU Charter"

You have to wonder why the EU is so adament about reading your private chats, right?

items.title:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

I am always excited by the growing and highly visible relevance, appearance, and applica...
items.content:
𝙈𝙮 𝙧𝙚𝙫𝙞𝙚𝙬:

I am always excited by the growing and highly visible relevance, appearance, and application of classical mathematical algorithms (particularly those related to learning and optimization) in practical real-world use cases. I have always loved mathematics and I easily recognized its practical applications (even in early education when my classmates thought it was boring and irrelevant). RL is one of those areas where the classical methods and theories (including game theory) are becoming both significant and critical, particularly (as in this book) in ubiquitous applications like Large Language Models, Generative AI, and AI Agents. The essential element of human feedback (in RLHF) further underscores the critical importance and value of these methods and algorithms. 

This book is an outstanding guide through these learning systems, applications, environments, policies, rewards, agents, and value propositions. Learn about RLHF, Q-learning, DPO (Direct Preference Optimization), PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization), RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback), and more.

I highly recommend this book for all readers - not only for developers and deployers of LLMs and AI Agents, but for everyone who will eventually (if not already) play a role in giving feedback to AI applications emerging in nearly all daily activities.

Disclosure: the publisher provided me with a free review copy of the book.

New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/WyEqNc9BnT

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learni...
items.content:
New release from @PacktDataML at https://t.co/WyEqNc9BnT

"A Practical Guide to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)"

𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘂𝗺𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆:

RLHF is a powerful approach to AI alignment and human-centered machine learning. By combining reinforcement learning algorithms with human feedback signals, RLHF has become a key method for improving the safety, reliability, and alignment of large language models (LLMs).

This book begins with the foundations of reinforcement learning and policy optimization, including algorithms such as proximal policy optimization (PPO), and explains how reward models and human preference learning help fine-tune AI systems and generative AI models.

items.title: They actually did that! I really hoped they would use the time since the first trailer to reshoot so...
items.content:
They actually did that! I really hoped they would use the time since the first trailer to reshoot some scenes and make people happy, but they doubled down on the shitshow and released this last trailer!

Christopher, I replenished my popcorn supplies, but not for the movie. I'm getting ready to watch the public reaction to this masterpiece!

Comments under this new trailer keep being hilarious:

- I paid nothing to see this trailer and I already want my money back.

- First time I’ve ever walked out of a trailer.

- The face that emptied a thousand seats.

- "Yo daddy ain't comin' home boy!"  Homer, 725 BC.

- I'm surprised they forgot the rap music.

- "Official countdown" Is that a threat?.

- Came for the ratio, stayed for the comments.

- Helena of Detroit.

- I'm sure Homer himself would enjoy the movie. He was blind.

- “If they played this on a plane I would still walk out.”

- It's like one of those 2006 funny parodies but without the fun.

- Hollywood is fighting piracy by making movies that are not even worth pirating.

- The only thing missing is Pedro Pascal as one of the sirens.

- Watching this is like finding out the last work of art Leonardo da Vinci ever made was a banana taped to a wall.

- Thank you hollywood for spending hundreds of millions of dollars so I can have fun in the Youtube comment section.

- Oh look, it's the same actors that are in every single movie.

- Nice to see Matt Damon showing his range by playing Matt Damon with a beard.

- Universal left the comments open to troll Christopher Nolan.

- Sisyphus was cursed by the gods to watch this movie on repeat.

items.title:
/7 Two more things worth knowing.

None of this was hand-designed. J-space exists in base models bef...
items.content:
/7 Two more things worth knowing.

None of this was hand-designed. J-space exists in base models before fine-tuning, and post-training reshapes it toward an assistant that monitors itself. It emerged from ordinary training.

It's also a training lever. Anthropic trained models only on what they'd say if asked to reflect, never on the task, and honesty on the real task went up.

One caution the paper is firm on. This is functional access, reading and using information internally. It says nothing about subjective experience.

items.title:
🌍 20 million lives impacted every single year. 

That is the power of technology when it is purposef...
items.content:
🌍 20 million lives impacted every single year. 

That is the power of technology when it is purposefully engineered to serve humanity. In many low and middle-income countries, healthcare professionals face immense barriers such as unreliable internet, a lack of localized content, and language gaps that can delay critical care.

To bridge this gap, Techies Without Borders built an AI-powered experimental platform that acts as a localized "doctor's assistant." Frontline workers can now type in a medical query and receive an instant, evidence-based answer in their own language. Thanks to NVIDIA AI and accelerated computing, medical insights that used to take 5 agonizing minutes to load are now delivered in just 2 seconds. And because it runs locally on edge devices, a lack of internet connection is no longer a barrier to saving lives. This isn't just a technical achievement; it's a lifeline for clinics facing severe physician shortages.

items.title:
Linear Algebra and Optimization for Machine Learning [516-page textbook]: https://t.co/s6oCEvVMKS
—
...
items.content:
Linear Algebra and Optimization for Machine Learning [516-page textbook]: https://t.co/s6oCEvVMKS
—
This textbook is organized around its 2 main themes:
[Amazon Summary]

1. Linear algebra and its applications: The chapters focus on the basics of linear algebra together with their common applications to singular value decomposition, matrix factorization, similarity matrices (kernel methods), and graph analysis. Numerous machine learning applications have been used as examples, such as spectral clustering, kernel-based classification, and outlier detection. The tight integration of linear algebra methods with examples from machine learning differentiates this book from generic volumes on linear algebra. The focus is clearly on the most relevant aspects of linear algebra for machine learning and to teach readers how to apply these concepts.

2. Optimization and its applications: Much of machine learning is posed as an optimization problem in which we try to maximize the accuracy of regression and classification models. The “parent problem” of optimization-centric machine learning is least-squares regression. Interestingly, this problem arises in both linear algebra and optimization, and is one of the key connecting problems of the two fields. Least-squares regression is also the starting point for support vector machines, logistic regression, and recommender systems. Furthermore, the methods for dimensionality reduction and matrix factorization also require the development of optimization methods. A general view of optimization in computational graphs is discussed together with its applications to back propagation in neural networks.

items.title:
Selfie-Statistic 

Facebook memory from an unknown year! 
 
92 million selfies are taken everyday, a...
items.content:
Selfie-Statistic 

Facebook memory from an unknown year! 
 
92 million selfies are taken everyday, accounting for 1.7% of all the photos taken everyday (5 Billion selfies per day), about 2.1 trillion per year! 
Over 40% of millennials have published a selfie at least once.
Over 82% of young adults have taken a selfie.
Individuals spend 54 hours a year (or 7 minutes everyday) taking selfies.
People smile in most of the selfies. 
The least smiles in selfies can be found in Eastern Europe (53%) - Bulgaria, Poland,  Romania, Belarus, Czech Republic, Hungary, Moldova.
22.1% of respondents edit their selfies
An average selfie-taker is 24 years old.

items.title:
What stood out:

1⃣ The November MCP spec update made durable tasks a requirement, not a nice-to-hav...
items.content:
What stood out:

1⃣ The November MCP spec update made durable tasks a requirement, not a nice-to-have. Tasks can now outlive the client, the network, and even the MCP server itself. The catch is that once a task is running, the client can't send it new information.
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: Highlights that the November MCP spec update made durable tasks mandatory so tasks can outlive client, network and server, with the caveat that running tasks cannot receive new input.
items.reasoning: Specific, informative point about MCP spec evolution relevant to agent infrastructure, though low reach.
items.summary_html: Highlights that the November MCP spec update made durable tasks mandatory so tasks can outlive client, network and server, with the caveat that running tasks cannot receive new input.
items.content_html:
What stood out:

1⃣ The November MCP spec update made durable tasks a requirement, not a nice-to-have. Tasks can now outlive the client, the network, and even the MCP server itself. The catch is that once a task is running, the client can't send it new information.

items.title: Three out of three. I recently read 3 articles from completely different sources & topic areas (fina...
items.content: Three out of three. I recently read 3 articles from completely different sources & topic areas (finance, local community news, home utilities), and the instances of incorrect grammar and other obviously unedited content were unmistakably due to negligence by the publications’ editors. Is this due to human fault or to human absence in exchange for AI doing their jobs?

Address real-world challenges such as imbalanced datasets, missing data, and high-dimensional inputs

items.title: 3⃣ Durability covers more than retries and state management. Imagine a tool that pays quarterly invo...
items.content: 3⃣ Durability covers more than retries and state management. Imagine a tool that pays quarterly invoice installments over a year, pausing between payments and resuming on durable timers that survive failures. If any piece of that chain isn't durable, the whole thing falls apart.
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: Explains that durability covers more than retries, using an example of installment payments over a year on durable timers.
items.reasoning: Concrete technical illustration of durable execution, but almost no engagement.
items.summary_html: Explains that durability covers more than retries, using an example of installment payments over a year on durable timers.
items.content_html: 3⃣ Durability covers more than retries and state management. Imagine a tool that pays quarterly invoice installments over a year, pausing between payments and resuming on durable timers that survive failures. If any piece of that chain isn't durable, the whole thing falls apart.

items.title: The Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST = deep, comprehensive, systematic, continuous 10-year "mov...
items.content:
The Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST = deep, comprehensive, systematic, continuous 10-year "movie" of the southern sky with a massive telescope and world's largest digital camera, producing 100's of petabytes of data) has begun! Congratulations to all the @VRubinObs staff — astronomers, engineers, education/outreach specialists, technicians, administrators, donors, external contributors, science collaborators, and more!

Read about it: https://t.co/LInlBY1E2I

Press release: https://t.co/PDvgJ9WqpU

I worked on the LSST design & development project for almost 15 years before retiring from academia. See some of the LSST-related research papers that I have contributed to: https://t.co/DUdqsbJwzq

items.title:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”
 
The real story was what AI ...
items.content:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”
 
The real story was what AI requires to be useful at enterprise scale.
 
AI depends on data.
 
Data depends on the network.
 
And under pressure, the question is not:
 
“Can the model find a pattern?”
 
It is:
“Can the right data reach the right systems in time to support operational decision-making?”
 
That distinction matters.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues the real enterprise AI question is whether the right data can reach the right systems in time, not whether a model can find a pattern.
items.reasoning: A cleaner distilled point on data pipelines under load, but still part of a low-engagement sponsored thread.
items.summary_html: Argues the real enterprise AI question is whether the right data can reach the right systems in time, not whether a model can find a pattern.
items.content_html:
At the PGA Championship, the real story was not just “AI at an event.”

The real story was what AI requires to be useful at enterprise scale.

AI depends on data.

Data depends on the network.

And under pressure, the question is not:

“Can the model find a pattern?”

It is:

“Can the right data reach the right systems in time to support operational decision-making?”

That distinction matters.

items.title:
The Operations Command Center is where this gets especially interesting.
 
Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G ...
items.content:
The Operations Command Center is where this gets especially interesting.
 
Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, operational data moved across connected workflows throughout the event footprint.
 
Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process.
 
That combination matters.
 
T-Mobile owns the network and connectivity layer.
 
CapTech owns the AI layer.
 
Together, that creates the enterprise model:
Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale

items.content_html:
The Operations Command Center is where this gets especially interesting.

Powered by T-Mobile’s 5G network, operational data moved across connected workflows throughout the event footprint.

Powered by CapTech’s AI, patterns surfaced earlier in the process.

That combination matters.

T-Mobile owns the network and connectivity layer.

CapTech owns the AI layer.

Together, that creates the enterprise model:

Network infrastructure plays a major role in how AI performs at scale

As a @TMobileBusiness Partner, I think this is the CIO conversation more companies need to have.

items.content_html:
@sigmaletter In my past, I helped Microsoft change its public image by being part of a site called Channel 9, which really changed how people viewed the company. I'm looking for fun, different, and novel projects like that.

There are a bunch of sponsorship opportunities. For example, I have an AI with a million posts in its database that can write a wide variety of reports. I could generate custom reports for people, or even write movie scripts, among other things.

Ultimately, I'm just looking for fun, creative ideas that go beyond simply selling posts.

AI performance is not just ...
items.content:
The PGA Championship is a useful reminder for every technology leader:
 
AI performance is not just a software issue.
 
It is an infrastructure issue.
 
Before asking what AI can automate, predict, or recommend, leaders should ask whether their network can support those decisions when everything is running at once.
 
What would your AI strategy look like under heavy operational demand?
Read it here: https://t.co/hPcxCSioU9

items.title:
The PGA Championship showed what modern enterprise operations increasingly look like.
 
· Thousands ...
items.content:
The PGA Championship showed what modern enterprise operations increasingly look like.
 
· Thousands of people.
· Dozens of systems.
· Multiple business-critical workflows.
· All running at once.
 
That sounds like a major sports event, but it also sounds like:
 
→ a hospital during peak demand
→ an airport during disruption
→ a university campus on game day
→ a manufacturing site with connected operations
→ a logistics hub managing connected operations
 
Different environments, same infrastructure question.

items.title:
Never ever buy a Daikin AC!

They limit how many times you can change them to just 150 times per day...
items.content:
Never ever buy a Daikin AC!

They limit how many times you can change them to just 150 times per day!

We have 7 ACs in the house so that's a limit of 21 instructions per day 😂 completely arbitrary btw, makes no sense, just classic Japanese software design

(I didn't buy it, it came with the house, and I've actively been ripping them out and replacing them with Mitsubishi)

Other good brands for ACs are LG, Hisense and Haier, can recommend!

items.summary: levelsio rants that Daikin ACs limit setting changes to 150 per day and recommends other brands, framing it as poor Japanese software design.

The Rubin Observatory @VRubinObs was named after Vera Rubin, to honor her brilliant career: https://t.co/NdwsMliQwd

Over 40 years ago, I was a Carnegie Fellow as a postdoc at @CarnegieScience. Vera Rubin was my mentor. She provided scientific guidance to me, involved me in some of her research projects, and helped me to get my first permanent job (as scientist at @NASA's @HubbleTelescope @stsci).
https://t.co/qXj7BlWQhm

items.content:
2 mg Melatonin is 10x too much

Melatonin is oddly sold in 10-100x too high doses

The right dose for Melatonin is about 0.2-0.3mg or 200-300mcg

Very very low taken an hour before sleep to give your natural melatonin production a kickstart, not replace it!

We’re deploying AI into some of the world’s most remote environments:

→ Oil rigs
→ Mining sites
→ Offshore platforms

Connected operations rely on operational data and resilient connectivity:

→ Sensors detect gas leaks
→ AI monitors pressure systems
→ Autonomous vehicles move heavy equipment

When connectivity becomes inconsistent...
Operational visibility can become more difficult to maintain.

That’s exactly the challenge SuperBroadband from @TMobileBusiness is designed to address - integrating 5G and Starlink connectivity to support resilient operations in remote environments.

items.title:
Here’s the mistake I see over and over:

Companies invest heavily in:

→ AI
→ Automation
→ Smart inf...
items.content:
Here’s the mistake I see over and over:

Companies invest heavily in:

→ AI
→ Automation
→ Smart infrastructure

But ignore the foundation.

Reality:
AI-enabled operations increasingly depend on resilient network infrastructure.

And in remote environments, resilient connectivity is becoming increasingly important.

That’s why integrated connectivity solutions like SuperBroadband - combining 5G and Starlink connectivity - are becoming increasingly important for modern operations.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Sponsored post claiming companies overinvest in AI while ignoring resilient network foundations, promoting a broadband product.
items.reasoning: Repetitive partner marketing with very low engagement.
items.summary_html: Sponsored post claiming companies overinvest in AI while ignoring resilient network foundations, promoting a broadband product.
items.content_html:
Here’s the mistake I see over and over:

→ AI

→ Automation

→ Smart infrastructure

And in remote environments, resilient connectivity is becoming increasingly important.

That’s why integrated connectivity solutions like SuperBroadband - combining 5G and Starlink connectivity - are becoming increasingly important for modern operations.

items.title: @pbertrand_dev @DanielLockyer "He's missing the point: Gluconeogenesis is a normal, demand-driven pr...
items.content: @pbertrand_dev @DanielLockyer "He's missing the point: Gluconeogenesis is a normal, demand-driven process on low-carb days—it doesn't mean your protein was "wasted." With 174g intake, you still had ample amino acids left for muscle repair; the body doesn't preferentially burn muscle when dietary protein is plentiful."

With 174g intake, you still had ample amino acids left for muscle repair; the body doesn't preferentially burn muscle when dietary protein is plentiful."

items.title:
@pbertrand_dev @DanielLockyer Nope:

"Gluconeogenesis pulls from the amino acid pool, and at 174g pr...
items.content:
@pbertrand_dev @DanielLockyer Nope:

"Gluconeogenesis pulls from the amino acid pool, and at 174g protein yesterday, your pool was massive. 

GNG demand on a day like yours might be ~50–80g of glucose-equivalent, your intake had room to feed both repair and glucose production. 

This is exactly why protein targets go up on low-carb diets: the surplus protein is partly there to be burned as fuel so muscle tissue isn't. 

If you'd eaten 100g protein on the same day, his warning would land squarely."
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: levelsio posts a detailed gluconeogenesis and protein argument about muscle preservation on low-carb days.
items.reasoning: Off-topic health argument with no AI relevance.
items.summary_html: levelsio posts a detailed gluconeogenesis and protein argument about muscle preservation on low-carb days.
items.content_html:
@pbertrand_dev @DanielLockyer Nope:

"Gluconeogenesis pulls from the amino acid pool, and at 174g protein yesterday, your pool was massive.

GNG demand on a day like yours might be ~50–80g of glucose-equivalent, your intake had room to feed both repair and glucose production.

This is exactly why protein targets go up on low-carb diets: the surplus protein is partly there to be burned as fuel so muscle tissue isn't.

If you'd eaten 100g protein on the same day, his warning would land squarely."

items.title:
ACP Pistols in 2024! 

Facebook Repost from yesterday! 

Developed just before the outbreak of the f...
items.content:
ACP Pistols in 2024! 

Facebook Repost from yesterday! 

Developed just before the outbreak of the first world war, the ACP quickly rose to prominence as the most widely used pistol cartridges globally. Being the longest-serving sidearm cartridge in the United States of America, it was only surpassed in popularity by the 9mm. The .45 ACP has always captivated shooters with its incredible power, exceptional performance, and deep-rooted nostalgia. Although this classic auto cartridge has had its time in the spotlight, its rich history and the wide range of top-notch .45 ACP pistols that are still going strong promise to keep its legacy alive for years to come.

Best Classic Pistol: 1911 A1 
Best Modern 1911: Nighthawk Custom Fire hawk 
Best Semi-Custom 1911: Wilson Combat ACP 
Best Concealed-Carry 1911: Springfield Armory TRP 
Best Gamer .45 ACP: H&K USP Tactical 
Best Workhorse .45 ACP: Glock G21 Gen 5 MOS 
Springfield XD-S Mod 2 OSP 
Sig Sauer P320 Compact 
FN FNX-45 Tactical
Best Classic is The issue 1911 A1

The 1911 A1 is indeed iconic and holds a special place in the history of firearms. Its longevity and service record are a testament to its reliability and effectiveness as a sidearm. It's fascinating to see how a design from over a century ago continues to be revered for its performance and impact.
Best Modern 1911: Nighthawk Custom Fire Hawk
The Nighthawk Custom Fire Hawk is truly a top-of-the-line 1911 pistol. Its impeccable craftsmanship and high-performance features definitely make it a standout choice for anyone seeking the best in terms of both aesthetics and functionality. The attention to detail in its design and the purpose behind every aspect of the pistol truly set it apart. From the scalloped grip panels ensuring a solid grip to the seamless integration of the muzzle compensator with the overall frame and slide, it's clear that this pistol is a masterful work of art and engineering.
Best Semi-Custom 1911: Combat ACP
The Combat ACP is an outstanding semi custom ACP pistol. With the ability for customers to select from a range of options, it offers a personalized touch while still being a production gun. Its performance and features made it a top picks in the 1911 testing.
Best Concealed-Carry-1911: Springfield Armory TRP CC
Looking for a 1911 platform for everyday concealed carry? Consider Springfield's new TRP Carry Contour. These single-stack compact 1911s boast a large barrel, a forged slide, and a lightweight aluminum frame. The standout feature is the bobbed frame, eliminating potential clothing printing or discomfort. Despite this design, the pistol still provides ample space for a solid, recoil-controlling grip.

Best ACP Pistol for Gamers: H&K USP Tactical

It's true that nostalgia often plays a role in your preference for certain guns, as seen with the enduring popularity of the 1911. For shooters who came of age in the 90s, the H&K Mark 23 and USP Tactical are likely to stir up fond memories. The Mark 23 gained fame for its association with the Navy Seals during that era — a detail that captivated many of you in your youth. The USP, introduced as a slightly more compact and lighter pistol, shares many similarities with its iconic counterpart.

Best Workhorse: Glock Gen 5 - G21

When it comes to top-notch ACP pistols, the Glock stands out as a leading choice in the realm of striker-fired firearms. Initially launched in 1990, following the 10 mm Glock, the G21 has a well-established track record of reliable performance and has been widely adopted by numerous law enforcement agencies. In 2023, the platform received significant enhancements with the introduction of the Gen 5 MOS edition.

PS: Greeting from Buellton!
